original_abstract: Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technology allows for rapid quantitation of cellular metabolites, with metabolites identified by mass spectrometry and chromatographic retention time. Recently, with the development of rapid scanning high-resolution high accuracy mass spectrometers and the desire for high throughput screening, minimal or no chromatographic separation has become increasingly popular. When analyzing complex cellular extracts, however, the lack of chromatographic separation could potentially result in misannotation of structurally related metabolites. Here, we show that, even using electrospray ionization, a soft ionization method, in-source fragmentation generates unwanted byproducts of identical mass to common metabolites. For example, nucleotide-triphosphates generate nucleotide-diphosphates, and hexose-phosphates generate triose-phosphates. We evaluated yeast intracellular metabolite extracts and found more than 20 cases of in-source fragments that mimic common metabolites. Accordingly, chromatographic separation is required for accurate quantitation of many common cellular metabolites.
humanized_abstract: We are interested in measuring the levels of cellular metabolites using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) in a very high throughput fashion. Metabolites are identified by their mass spectrums and the chromatographic retention time. Recently with the advent of rapid scanning high resolution and high accuracy mass spectrometers, many people are trying to get by with as little as possible chromatography, particularly with source such as ESI which is a soft ionization method. Although chromatography may not be necessary or desired for the analysis of pure compounds, we have found that the complex cellular extracts that we have for LC-MS analysis require some form of chromatography in order for the compounds to be accurately measured, and that the lack of chromatography can lead to the mis-annotation of structurally related compounds (i.e. the in-source fragmentation products of a compound will have the same mass as the compound, and conversely, the parent compound will have the same mass as its fragmentation products). For example nucleotide-triphosphates fragment into nucleotide-diphosphates and the hexose-phosphates fragment into triose-phosphates. We have measured the yeast intracellular metabolites, and there are over 20 cases of in which the in-source fragments are of metabolites that are of major interest. In order to measure the metabolites of interest accurately in our experiments, chromatography must be used.
